did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated a deep learning-based artificial intelligence model, specifically a convolutional neural network (CNN) using a ResNet34 backbone, to automatically detect basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) images. The model achieved an AUC of 89.7% at the stack level and 88.3% at the lesion level, demonstrating its use for diagnostic classification in dermatology.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes the development and evaluation of a deep learning-based artificial intelligence model for detecting basal cell carcinoma in reflectance confocal microscopy images. It presents original experimental results, including a retrospective study of 66 lesions from 52 patients, training of a convolutional neural network, five-fold cross-validation, and external validation on an independent dataset from international collaborators, reporting specific performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity).

title: Deep Learning for Basal Cell Carcinoma Detection for Reflectance Confocal Microscopy
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated a deep learning-based artificial intelligence model to automatically detect basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) images. The researchers utilized a convolutional neural network with a ResNet34 backbone, trained on 267 RCM image stacks from 66 lesions in 52 patients at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center. The model's performance was assessed using five-fold cross-validation and external validation on an independent dataset from international collaborators in Italy and Australia. The AI model achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 89.7% at the stack level and 88.3% at the lesion level, performing on par with expert RCM readers. Furthermore, the model demonstrated generalizability by achieving an AUC of 86.1% on the external test set. These results suggest that automated decision support systems for BCC detection in RCM have the potential to optimize skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target of the study; 41 of 66 lesions (62.1%) had a diagnosis of BCC on final histopathology. The model was trained to detect BCC vs not-BCC.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic label: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) vs. Not-BCC (NB)
all_task_types.details: The model was trained to classify each RCM image within a stack as BCC or not-BCC based on expert annotations. Image predictions were aggregated into stack-level and lesion-level predictions via max-pooling and average pooling, respectively. The task is binary classification, evaluated using ROC curves and AUC metrics (89.7% at stack level, 88.3% at lesion level).

datasets_used.name: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) Internal Dataset
datasets_used.description: A retrospective cohort of reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) images collected from 52 consecutive patients with clinically equivocal lesions at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (New York, NY, USA). The dataset consists of RCM stacks acquired using arm-mounted (Vivascope 1500) or handheld (Vivascope 3000) devices. Stacks containing malignant lesions other than BCC or low-quality stacks were excluded.

use.training: Used for training the convolutional neural network (CNN) via five-fold cross-validation. A total of 267 stacks were used in the cross-validation process.
use.validation: Used for validation via five-fold cross-validation. The final cross-validation performance was obtained by concatenating results from the validation split of each training fold. 131 stacks were used for blinded consensus reading by human experts.
use.testing: Used for internal testing via five-fold cross-validation. The model achieved an AUC of 89.7% at the stack level and 88.3% at the lesion level on this dataset.
use.other: An ensemble of models was trained on the full cohort of 267 stacks for generalization testing.

datasets_used.name: International External Validation Dataset
datasets_used.description: An independent test set of RCM stacks collected from international collaborators in Italy and Australia to assess generalization. It includes stacks from three institutions: Università degli Studi di Modena e Reggio Emilia (Modena, Italy), University of Modena and Reggio Emilia (Reggio Emilia, Italy), and Sydney Melanoma Diagnostic Centre (Sydney, Australia). The dataset was labeled at the stack level with histopathology confirmation.

use.testing: Used for external validation to test the generalization performance of the model. The model achieved an AUC of 86.1% on this dataset.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To train the predictive model and evaluate its performance on the internal dataset by splitting the data into five folds at the stack level.
To generate performance curves and calculate the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for the model and human experts.
To calculate 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the performance metrics (sensitivity and specificity) of the model and human experts.
To report the uncertainty around sensitivity and specificity estimates for both the AI model and the panel of human experts.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) to compare the AI model's performance against human experts. Performance was compared using Area Under the Curve (AUC) values and 95% Confidence Intervals (CI) calculated via bootstrapping, with the conclusion based on the overlap of CIs and comparable AUCs rather than statistical significance testing.

stack_level_performance.details: Performance on 131 stacks with blinded consensus ground truth. The model achieved an AUC of 90.1% on the entire dataset of 259 stacks, but the comparison with experts was restricted to the 131 stacks.

expert_comparison_stack_level.details: Blinded consensus of expert RCM readers on 131 stacks.

lesion_level_performance.details: Performance on 62 lesions (aggregated via average pooling). The AUC for the full validation cohort of 62 lesions was 90.0%.

Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (.notes: The model's performance was at least comparable to human experts. The CI analysis for the model ROC and experts' performance showed some overlap.

International External Validation Datase.source: Università degli Studi di Modena e Reggio Emilia (Modena, Italy), University of Modena and Reggio Emilia (Reggio Emilia, Italy), and Sydney Melanoma Diagnostic Centre (Sydney, Australia)

stack_level_performance.model: Ensemble of models trained on full internal cohort

International External Validation Datase.notes: Demonstrated generalizability with only a modest drop in performance compared to the cross-validation result.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on ResNet34 backbone

architecture_details.modifications: Two additional residual blocks added to increase the receptive field of the last feature map
architecture_details.feature_maps: 32x32 pixels-wide feature representation after final convolutional layer; 8x8 pixels feature representation after added blocks
architecture_details.heads: Two independent average pooling and fully connected classification layers connected to the 32-pixel and 8-pixel feature representations respectively
architecture_details.loss_function: Sum of cross-entropy losses from each classification layer during training; only the last classification layer (8 pixels) used for prediction at test time

training_details.learning_rate: Initial learning rate of 0.001, annealed every 20 epochs by a factor of 10
training_details.augmentation: Random 90-degree rotations, random horizontal flips, intensity jittering, and Gaussian blur
training_details.validation_strategy: Five-fold cross-validation at the stack level

classification_or_output_head.layers: Fully connected classification layers connected to average pooling layers

Exclusion of stacks containing malignant lesions other than BCC
Exclusion of stacks showing background normal skin (obtained outside lesional area)
Removal of Suspicious (S) images from the training set (kept for inference)
Removal of Bad Quality (BQ) images from the training set
Image augmentation during training: random 90-degree rotations, random horizontal flips, intensity jittering, and Gaussian blur

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training as a geometric transform.
Applied during training as a geometric transform.
Applied during training as a color/intensity transform.
Applied during training as a filtering transform.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning-based artificial intelligence model achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 89.7% at the stack level and 88.3% at the lesion level for detecting basal cell carcinoma (BCC) in reflectance confocal microscopy (RCM) images.
The AI model's performance was found to be on par with that of expert RCM readers.

The model demonstrated generalizability and reproducibility by achieving an AUC of 86.1% on an external test set from international collaborators in Italy and Australia, showing only a modest drop in performance compared to the internal cross-validation results.

The study provides evidence that the clinical deployment of decision support systems for BCC detection in RCM images has the potential to optimize the evaluation and diagnosis of patients with skin cancer.
The model learned the relationship between depth and BCC occurrence without explicit depth training, with positive predictions for BCC tending to occur in deeper sections of the skin.
Occlusion map analysis confirmed that the model correctly identified relevant histological features, such as tumor nodules for BCC and benign structures like elongated cords for non-BCC lesions.
important_remarks: The study has a relatively small sample size (66 lesions from 52 patients), which is a major limitation; the authors acknowledge the need for validation on a larger cohort.
The internal dataset was collected retrospectively from a single center (Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center), potentially introducing selection bias.

The model was trained on small single field-of-view (FOV) stacks (0.5-1 mm²) rather than large mosaics, which improves applicability to handheld RCM devices but may introduce sampling errors within lesions.

External validation was performed on a small independent dataset (53 stacks from 34 lesions) from international collaborators in Italy and Australia, limiting the robustness of generalizability claims.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was used to compare the AI model against human experts; performance comparison relied on overlapping confidence intervals.
The study excluded stacks containing malignant lesions other than BCC (e.g., melanoma) from the training set, which limits the model's ability to distinguish BCC from other malignancies.
The model learned to associate BCC predictions with deeper skin sections without explicit depth training, which may reflect a learned heuristic rather than purely histological features.
False positives were observed in cases where hair follicles mimicked BCC tumor nodules, indicating a potential vulnerability to specific benign structures.
